examples.title: Family Relationship Program
examples.description: Basic Prolog program demonstrating facts, rules, and queries for family relationships
examples.code:
% Facts - Basic relationships
father(john, mary).
father(john, tom).
mother(susan, mary).
mother(susan, tom).

% Rules - Derived relationships
parent(X, Y) :- father(X, Y).
parent(X, Y) :- mother(X, Y).

sibling(X, Y) :- parent(Z, X), parent(Z, Y), X \= Y.

grandparent(X, Y) :- parent(X, Z), parent(Z, Y).

% Queries (to test in Prolog console)
% ?- parent(john, Who).
% ?- sibling(mary, tom).
examples.explanation: This program shows basic Prolog structure. Facts define parent-child relationships. Rules derive new relationships (parent, sibling, grandparent) using the :– operator. The comma (,) means logical AND. Queries ask questions to the knowledge base.

examples.title: List Processing - Member and Length
examples.description: Recursive list processing predicates in Prolog
examples.code:
% member/2 - Check if element is in list
member(X, [X|_]).
member(X, [_|T]) :- member(X, T).

% length/2 - Find length of list
length([], 0).
length([_|T], N) :- length(T, M), N is M + 1.

% append/3 - Concatenate two lists
append([], L, L).
append([H|T], L, [H|R]) :- append(T, L, R).

% reverse/2 - Reverse a list
reverse([], []).
reverse([H|T], R) :- reverse(T, TR), append(TR, [H], R).
examples.explanation: These are fundamental list processing predicates. member/2 uses pattern matching to check if X appears in the list. length/2 counts elements recursively. append/3 concatenates lists by decomposing the first list. All use recursion with base cases.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for member, O(n²) for reverse

examples.title: Equivalent Python Implementation
examples.description: Python equivalent of Prolog facts and rules for comparison
examples.explanation: This shows the imperative equivalent of the Prolog program. While Prolog automatically handles search and backtracking, Python requires explicit loops and conditions. The declarative Prolog is more concise for knowledge representation.

examples.title: C Implementation of List Member
examples.description: C implementation showing imperative vs declarative approach
examples.explanation: This C implementation contrasts with Prolog's approach. The imperative version typically returns one answer, while Prolog finds all solutions through backtracking. Note how explicit loops replace Prolog's implicit search.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for member, O(n²) to find all occurrences

examples.title: Tower of Hanoi in Prolog
examples.description: Recursive solution to Tower of Hanoi puzzle
examples.code:
% Tower of Hanoi in Prolog
% hanoi(N, Source, Auxiliary, Target)

% Base case: 0 disks - nothing to move
hanoi(0, _, _, _) :- !.

% Recursive case
hanoi(N, A, B, C) :- 
    N > 0,
    M is N - 1,
    % Move N-1 disks from Source to Auxiliary
    hanoi(M, A, C, B),
    % Move largest disk from Source to Target
    write('Move disk from '), write(A), 
    write(' to '), write(C), nl,
    % Move N-1 disks from Auxiliary to Target
    hanoi(M, B, A, C).

% Example queries:
% ?- hanoi(3, left, middle, right).
examples.explanation: This classic recursive algorithm shows Prolog's power for problem solving. The base case stops recursion (0 disks). The recursive case breaks the problem into three parts: move N-1 to auxiliary, move largest to target, move N-1 to target. The cut (!) after base case prevents unnecessary backtracking.
examples.timeComplexity: O(2^n) - exponential as expected for Hanoi
